patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the way memory cgroup (memcg) charges kmem pages and slab objects against per-node object cgroups (objcgs). It introduces a new helper function __current_obj_cgroup(int nid) that allows callers to specify the target node for the objcg lookup. The patch then uses this helper in two places: __memcg_kmem_charge_page() and __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook(). This change ensures that kmem pages and slab objects are charged against the correct per-node objcg, even when the allocating CPU's node differs from the allocation's node.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a metadata charge size calculation error in the Linux kernel's percpu code. The function pcpu_obj_full_size() was using an incorrect size for the obj_exts metadata, which is a vector of struct pcpuobj_ext. The fix involves changing the size calculation to use sizeof(struct pcpuobj_ext) instead of sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *). This patch addresses a bug introduced in commit 8f30d2660a38 and is part of a larger series of patches.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series splits the memcg charging and kmem accounting in percpu allocations, decoupling them to prevent overcharging of memory cgroups. The pre-alloc hook now reserves pages for limit enforcement without updating kmem stats, while the post-alloc hook accounts kmem and places sub-page remainders into the obj stock after allocation succeeds.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with overcharging of memory cgroups due to the decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting, but noted that the window is small and may not be worth noting. Suggested using PFN_UP(x) or DIV_ROUND_UP(x, PAGE_SIZE) instead of PAGE_ALIGN(x) >> PAGE_SHIFT.
review_comments.raw_body:
> This is preparatory patch for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem
> accounting.

Hi Alex, I hope you are doing well. Thank you for the series!
And sorry that I took so long to review. I'm slowly going through the
series, so I expect to leave more comments in the future. 

> Percpu allocations charge memory before knowing which NUMA nodes the
> pages will land on. So we need to decouple the memcg charging from the
> kmem accounting:
> 
> 1. In the pre-alloc hook, obj_cgroup_precharge() reserves pages for
>    memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats.
> 2. In the post-alloc hook, obj_cgroup_account_kmem() accounts kmem
>    and places the sub-page remainder into the obj stock after the
>    allocation succeeds.
> 
> Because of that decoupling, we must not rely on the stock in the
> precharge function and always charge the necessary pages that will
> be accounted after the allocations happened.

I was thinking about this, I think the reality of the sitaution is
actually a bit better than the description makes it seem. We're still
using stock, just a different level so we're not losing any
fastpath at all. Previously we would use the objcg stock to do the
charge quickly, but if we're going straight to memcg charging, we'll
be using the memcg stock instead. Maybe a clarification here that
we're not relying on the obj_stock would help your case here : -)

> That means we may
> temporarily overcharge the memcg but the obj_stock draining will get
> things back to normal.

Nice, this is quite clean. We might hit memcg limits faster because
we overcharge up to PAGE_SIZE bytes but... I'm not sure if this is even
worth noting since the window is so small. 

I think in all the places where you use PAGE_ALIGN(x) >> PAGE_SHIFT
we can instead use PFN_UP(x)? Or maybe DIV_ROUND_UP(x, PAGE_SIZE)?
Just thought that this way we could avoid some of the two-line function
calls ; -)

> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Anyways I think the split looks clean and makes sense to me. Sashiko
raised no concerns either, just wanted to note some nits. I imagine
there will be a v3 in the future? I think this patch looks good but
I will wait until the next version to leave my signatures in case
some of the context changes.

Thanks again, Alex! I hope you have a great day,
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track MEMCG_KMEM statistics for NUMA nodes. It removes the MEMCG_KMEM field and wires generic functions to use the new per-node accounting. The patch does not convert kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is done in subsequent patches. This change aims to improve memory management and tracking on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting for percpu allocations. It decouples the memcg charging from kmem accounting, allowing the post-alloc hook to account pages on their actual node rather than just the best-effort placement node. The patch also optimizes the accounting process by batching per node instead of per page, reducing the number of memcg work operations proportional to the number of nodes. Additionally, it precharges enough pages to cover the worst-case scenario where the allocation spans all nodes, ensuring that the per-node credit never runs short.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata in the Linux kernel's mm/percpu.c file. The obj_exts array is vmalloc'd and its pages may reside on different nodes, so it walks these pages to add each page's slice to the correct node's byte accumulation. This ensures that each node is rounded up to whole pages only once, across payload and metadata combined.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] mm: percpu: skip the per-cpu node walk on single-node systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the per-cpu memory allocation by skipping a time-consuming walk of possible CPU nodes on single-node systems. The existing code would perform this walk to determine the NUMA node for each backing page, but since all pages are already on the same node in a single-node system, this step is unnecessary and only adds overhead. A new fast path has been added to pcpu_memcg_accumulate() that directly attributes the allocation to the first online node when there's only one online node, thus avoiding the O(num_possible_cpus) walk.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates zswap and zsmalloc to use per-node object cgroup (obj_cgroup) for kernel memory (kmem) accounting, attributing compressed page charges to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that straddle pages across multiple nodes, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch adds a function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node for a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use this information. This change is necessary to ensure accurate kmem accounting for compressed pages.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that zs_handle_to_nid() does not acquire pool->lock like other handle-to-zspage paths, potentially causing a race condition between zswap's uncharge path and zspage migration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Does this need the same locking as the other handle-to-zspage paths?
zs_free() takes pool->lock before handle_to_obj() because zspage migration can
update or move the object behind the handle. This helper does the same decode
without the lock, so zswap's uncharge path can race migration and charge or
uncharge the wrong node, or observe transient zspage state.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested charging compressed page charges to the page's node for now, and then revisiting the correct attribution once Joshua's patch series is in.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can we just charge it to the page's node for now? Once Joshua's patch
series is in, we can correctly charge the node owning the data :)

FWIW, this is how these zswap entries are organized in the LRU too -
following to the OG page's node.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged that the patch was incomplete and failed to correctly account for entries straddling pages on different nodes, but did not provide a plan for addressing this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

On 6/26/26 16:32, Usama Arif wrote:

---

You're totally right, I missed this, thanks!

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledges that the current per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc has inaccuracies but believes using the original page's node is closer to reality, despite being incorrect.

Author acknowledges a need to address the issue of compressed data being on a different node than the original page, but does not provide a specific plan for addressing it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

On 6/26/26 20:36, Nhat Pham wrote:

---

Even if this patch accounting is incorrect, it is close to reality, 
using the original page's node would give results that are really off no?

---

Oh, we should do something about that right? Because the compressed data 
is not necessarily on the original page's node.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the current policy is same-node-first for compressed data, which affects the accuracy of per-node kmem accounting and requested further analysis on the impact of fallbacks.

The reviewer noted that migration of a zsmalloc object requires moving a zswap entry from one LRU list to another, and expressed concern about the necessary synchronization method, specifically mentioning the need for taking LRU locks but questioning what else might be required.
review_comments.raw_body:
The current policy is same-node-first, i.e we prefer the same node as
the original page for the compressed data too:

https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/56e5a103a721d0ef139bba7ff3d3ada6c8217d5b

So the accuracy of this guesstimation depends on how much fallback we
had to do...

---

At initial placement? Yes, it's fixable.

But the bigger problem is on migration of a zsmalloc object. That
requires moving zswap entry from one lru list to another :) I'm not
sure what synchronization method is safe here - at minimum we need to
take the LRU locks, but what about incoming writeback, zswap load,
etc.?

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested extending zsmalloc's interface to return the initial node placement together with the handle in zs_malloc(), which would avoid acquiring the pool->lock again and reduce contention, especially during compaction.
review_comments.raw_body:
If we are to do this, is there a way to extend zsmalloc's interface so
that it returns the initial node placement together with the handle in
zs_malloc()?

That way, we can avoid going through the zsmalloc locks again. It's
quiet expensive, especially with compaction in the picture - the
pool->lock is a global rwlock :)

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the task_struct's objcg field to a per-node array of obj_cgroup pointers, allowing for efficient and accurate accounting of memory usage across multiple NUMA nodes. The change addresses issues with current_obj_cgroup() returning an objcg whose nid did not match the current CPU's node, defeating per-node vmstat batching and accounting hierarchy. The patch also introduces a new tagged pointer to store the array, ensuring atomic updates and preserving existing race-with-kmem_attach semantics.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 9/9] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch introduces per-node kmem accounting for zswap and zsmalloc, but its accuracy is questioned due to potential node changes during migration.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's accuracy, suggesting that even if it is close to reality, it may not be accurate due to node changes during migration.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Provided context about the current policy of same-node-first for compressed data and highlighted potential issues with node changes during migration, including synchronization concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 6:38 AM Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:
>
> Hi Nhat,
>
> On 6/26/26 20:36, Nhat Pham wrote:
> > On Fri, Jun 26, 2026 at 7:32 AM Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> >> On Fri, 26 Jun 2026 12:20:58 +0200 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:
> >>
> >>> Update zswap and zsmalloc to use per-node obj_cgroup for kmem
> >>> accounting, attributing compressed page charges to the correct
> >>> NUMA node.
> >>>
> >>> But actually, this is incomplete because it does not correctly account
> >>> for entries that straddle pages, those pages being possibly on 2 different
> >>> nodes.
> >>>
> >>> This will be correctly handled by Joshua in a different series [1].
> >>>
> >>> Link: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260311195153.4013476-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/ [1]
> >>> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> >>> ---
> >>>   include/linux/zsmalloc.h |  2 ++
> >>>   mm/zsmalloc.c            | 11 +++++++++++
> >>>   mm/zswap.c               | 19 ++++++++++++++++++-
> >>>   3 files changed, 31 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> >>>
> >>> diff --git a/include/linux/zsmalloc.h b/include/linux/zsmalloc.h
> >>> index 478410c880b1..30427f3fe232 100644
> >>> --- a/include/linux/zsmalloc.h
> >>> +++ b/include/linux/zsmalloc.h
> >>> @@ -50,6 +50,8 @@ void zs_obj_read_sg_end(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle);
> >>>   void zs_obj_write(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle,
> >>>                  void *handle_mem, size_t mem_len);
> >>>
> >>> +int zs_handle_to_nid(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle);
> >>> +
> >>>   extern const struct movable_operations zsmalloc_mops;
> >>>
> >>>   #endif
> >>> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> >>> index 83f5820c45f9..17f7403ebe77 100644
> >>> --- a/mm/zsmalloc.c
> >>> +++ b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> >>> @@ -1380,6 +1380,17 @@ static void obj_free(int class_size, unsigned long obj)
> >>>        mod_zspage_inuse(zspage, -1);
> >>>   }
> >>>
> >>> +int zs_handle_to_nid(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle)
> >>> +{
> >>> +     unsigned long obj;
> >>> +     struct zpdesc *zpdesc;
> >>> +
> >>> +     obj = handle_to_obj(handle);
> >>> +     obj_to_zpdesc(obj, &zpdesc);
> >>> +     return page_to_nid(zpdesc_page(zpdesc));
> >>> +}
> >>> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(zs_handle_to_nid);
> >> Does this need the same locking as the other handle-to-zspage paths?
> >> zs_free() takes pool->lock before handle_to_obj() because zspage migration can
> >> update or move the object behind the handle. This helper does the same decode
> >> without the lock, so zswap's uncharge path can race migration and charge or
> >> uncharge the wrong node, or observe transient zspage state.
> >>
> > Can we just charge it to the page's node for now? Once Joshua's patch
> > series is in, we can correctly charge the node owning the data :)
>
>
> Even if this patch accounting is incorrect, it is close to reality,
> using the original page's node would give results that are really off no?

The current policy is same-node-first, i.e we prefer the same node as
the original page for the compressed data too:

https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/56e5a103a721d0ef139bba7ff3d3ada6c8217d5b

So the accuracy of this guesstimation depends on how much fallback we
had to do...

>
>
> >
> > FWIW, this is how these zswap entries are organized in the LRU too -
> > following to the OG page's node.
>
>
> Oh, we should do something about that right? Because the compressed data
> is not necessarily on the original page's node.

At initial placement? Yes, it's fixable.

But the bigger problem is on migration of a zsmalloc object. That
requires moving zswap entry from one lru list to another :) I'm not
sure what synchronization method is safe here - at minimum we need to
take the LRU locks, but what about incoming writeback, zswap load,
etc.?

>
> Thanks,
>
> Alex
>

The current policy is same-node-first, i.e we prefer the same node as

https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/56e5a103a721d0ef139bba7ff3d3ada6c8217d5b

So the accuracy of this guesstimation depends on how much fallback we

But the bigger problem is on migration of a zsmalloc object. That
requires moving zswap entry from one lru list to another :) I'm not

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_inhibit_eb_writeback function to use GFP_NOWAIT instead of GFP_NOFS when allocating memory for inhibiting writeback on an extent buffer. This change is made because the allocation is already best effort and occurs while holding locks, making NOWAIT a suitable choice.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs readahead mechanism to use GFP_NOWAIT when reading tree blocks, allowing it to fail instead of blocking in direct reclaim if allocation fails. This change is intended to prevent painful stalls and lock contention caused by readahead getting stuck in direct reclaim.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch enables unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations in the Btrfs file system. It introduces a new preallocation struct to carry allocation and signal requirements, allowing callers to attempt NOWAIT allocations if supported by the allocator. If the initial allocation fails, it signals that preallocation is required, enabling the caller to perform the necessary allocation and retry with the preallocated resources. The patch also notes that two GFP_NOFS allocations remain under lock and cannot be preallocated due to limitations in the filemap xarray.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: add struct btrfs_eb_prealloc
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new structure called `struct btrfs_eb_prealloc` to carry the extent_buffer and btrfs_folio_state needed for NOFAIL allocations with retries outside the critical section. The existing allocation pathways are refactored to use this new struct, but no functional changes are made. Empty prealloc structs are wired in from callers. This is part of a larger series of patches to support NOFAIL allocations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: factor init_extent_buffer from __alloc_extent_buffer
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the initialization of extent buffers in Btrfs by separating it from the allocation process. The goal is to prepare for preallocating extent buffer data, which will allow for more efficient memory management. The patch introduces a new function `init_extent_buffer` that initializes an existing extent buffer, and modifies the existing `__alloc_extent_buffer` function to allocate the buffer first and then initialize it using the new function. This change enables the allocation of buffers in multiple code paths while still sharing initialization code.

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Thumshirn noted that the new init_extent_buffer function is being called from both __alloc_extent_buffer and alloc_extent_buffer, which he considers incorrect. He suggests either removing the call to init_extent_buffer in __alloc_extent_buffer or reverting back to calling __alloc_extent_buffer directly in alloc_extent_buffer.
review_comments.raw_body:
This looks wrong to me. Not the split out code, but how it is used in 
alloc_extent_buffer(). Either you keep calling __alloc_extent_buffer() 
in alloc_extent_buffer() OR don't call init_extent_buffer() in 
__alloc_extent_buffer(). I see this is an intermediate step (and I 
haven't looked at the rest of the series yet) but it looks wrong.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch's current implementation is not yet optimal, as alloc_extent_buffer should eventually call elsewhere, but left __alloc_extent_buffer for specific use cases and asked if it should be deleted.
review_comments.raw_body:
The idea is that you eventually (optionally) have the allocation happen
elsewhere, so alloc_extent_buffer should not call __alloc_extent_buffer
any more. That should be clearer in the second patch, I think.

I left __alloc_extent_buffer for btrfs_clone_extent_buffer() and
alloc_dummy_extent_buffer(). Would you prefer me to delete
__alloc_extent_buffer and open code it in those two callers?

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: Reviewer initially had concerns about the patch, but after reviewing the entire series, they found it made sense and apologized for their initial noise.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes looking at it again with the complete series applied it make more 
sense. Sorry for the noise.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses contention and stalls in the Linux kernel caused by btrfs extent_buffer allocations being made while holding locks. The patches modify the allocation of extent_buffers to use GFP_NOWAIT, which allows the allocation to fail and retry outside of the critical section with GFP_NOFS | GFP_NOFAIL. This approach is analogous to how extent_buffers are read, where locks must be dropped before accessing the data. Although the series does not completely eliminate lock-holding allocations, it significantly reduces contention by allowing small cached slab allocations to occur outside of the critical path.

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that alloc_extent_buffer() calls __alloc_extent_buffer() and init_extent_buffer(), which is inconsistent, and requested to either remove one of these calls or refactor the code
review_comments.raw_body:
This looks wrong to me. Not the split out code, but how it is used in 
alloc_extent_buffer(). Either you keep calling __alloc_extent_buffer() 
in alloc_extent_buffer() OR don't call init_extent_buffer() in 
__alloc_extent_buffer(). I see this is an intermediate step (and I 
haven't looked at the rest of the series yet) but it looks wrong.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the __alloc_extent_buffer function should not be called from alloc_extent_buffer, as it's meant to be used elsewhere; agreed to restructure in a future version (v2) and asked for feedback on deleting or open coding this function.
review_comments.raw_body:
The idea is that you eventually (optionally) have the allocation happen
elsewhere, so alloc_extent_buffer should not call __alloc_extent_buffer
any more. That should be clearer in the second patch, I think.

I left __alloc_extent_buffer for btrfs_clone_extent_buffer() and
alloc_dummy_extent_buffer(). Would you prefer me to delete
__alloc_extent_buffer and open code it in those two callers?

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: reviewer initially had concerns about the patch but after re-examining the complete series, they found it made sense and apologized for their previous noise
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes looking at it again with the complete series applied it make more 
sense. Sorry for the noise.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v3] smp: Use release stores for csd_lock_record() state

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 05/30] mm/rmap: update mm/interval_tree.c comments
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch updates comments in mm/interval_tree.c to improve code readability.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the clarity of function definitions and suggested that the code should be refactored to improve readability. Approved the patch as an improvement, but felt it was a Band-Aid solution rather than addressing the underlying API issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 05:41:16PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 12:01:02PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 01:23:16PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> > > Update the file comment to clarify that both file-backed and anonymous
> > > interval trees are provided, referencing the relevant data types for
> > > clarity.
> > >
> >
> > Isn't this self-evident by nature of the function definitions?
> > (one takes a vm_area_struct, the other takes an anon_vma_chain)
> 
> Well you see you're already hitting up on issues there, they both take an
> rb_root_cached and the vma_*() ones do not instantly scream 'file-backed' do
> they? As VMAs are obviously used for buth anon and file-backed...
> 
> But later patches fix this stuff :)
> 
> And I feel it's hard visually to see where one set of definitions end and
> another begins, which was really the motive for this, as trivial as it is!
> 

Fair enough, I scanned the rest initially but trying to wrap my head
around everything as i go through one by one.  Generally this really
screams "fix the apis" not "comment the bad ones" - but i suppose that's
the whole point here.

It's definitely an improvement either way.

Reviewed-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Clarified the intent of the patch and explained that it is a step towards eventually removing anon-related code from mm/interval_tree.c. Addressed Gregory's concerns about function definitions and acknowledged that the patch is an improvement, but not a complete solution.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 12:01:02PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 01:23:16PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> > Update the file comment to clarify that both file-backed and anonymous
> > interval trees are provided, referencing the relevant data types for
> > clarity.
> >
>
> Isn't this self-evident by nature of the function definitions?
> (one takes a vm_area_struct, the other takes an anon_vma_chain)

Well you see you're already hitting up on issues there, they both take an
rb_root_cached and the vma_*() ones do not instantly scream 'file-backed' do
they? As VMAs are obviously used for buth anon and file-backed...

But later patches fix this stuff :)

And I feel it's hard visually to see where one set of definitions end and
another begins, which was really the motive for this, as trivial as it is!

>
> > -	VM_BUG_ON_VMA(vma_start_pgoff(node) != vma_start_pgoff(prev), node);
> > +	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE_VMA(vma_start_pgoff(node) != vma_start_pgoff(prev), node);
> >
>
> For my own edification - I know not to add new BUG(), should I be
> converting BUG->WARN/something when i find them in areas i happen to be
> working in?

Yeah pretty much in all cases.

It's very rare that you'd want the kernel to definitely oops, and I can't think
of any circumstance where you'd only what that if CONFIG_DEBUG_VM was set :))

>
> ~Gregory

Cheers, Lorenzo

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch aims to fix automatic NUMA balancing for shmem, but Gregory Price expresses concerns about demotion and its impact on memory placement.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified potential problems with demotion and its impact on memory placement, suggesting a complete overhaul of the demotion mechanism.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Gregory's concerns and expressed willingness to address them, but also emphasized the complexity of mbinding in tiered mode.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 01:59:41PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 12:33:37PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > Neha reports that mapped shmem aren't considered for NUMA balancing,
> > noting convergence problems and bandwidth bottlenecking for cachelib
> > based workloads on tiered memory systems.
> > 
> > Looking at the code and going through the git history, this doesn't
> > actually seem intentional:
> > 
> > Commit fc3147245d19 ("mm: numa: Limit NUMA scanning to migrate-on-fault
> > VMAs") added a vma_policy_mof() gate to task_numa_work() so VMAs whose
> > policy lacks MPOL_F_MOF are skipped from NUMA balancing scans. The
> > motivation was a real usecase: Oracle was pinning shared segments with
> > mbind(MPOL_BIND) so trapping faults was both expensive and pointless.
> > 
> > The handling of NULL from vm_ops->get_policy, however, treated "user
> > explicitly opted out" the same as "user never specified anything." For
> > VMAs whose shared policy is absent - the common case for shmem - the
> > scan was disabled too.
> > 
> > This issue is old. It probably hurts less in conventional NUMA. But it's
> > very noticable on tiered systems, where entire tmpfs workingsets can get
> > stuck on lower-bandwidth memory.
> > 
> 
> Eugh.
> 
> Demotions don't care about mempolicy, so opting shmem out of NUMA
> balancing and mbind'ing on a tiered system is just full sadness.

Right, mbinding in tiered mode is a whole other ball of wax. I'm just
trying to make the default case work ;-)

> This is all just more evidence that demotion needs to be completely
> redone, it's creating a mess of undefined behavior for memory placement.

No argument from me.

> > Fix this by having vma_policy_mof() use __get_vma_policy() directly, and
> > thereby handle the fallback to task policy (-> preferred_node_policy()
> > has MPOL_F_MOF per default). Every other consumer of vm_ops->get_policy
> > already handles it this way, the scan-eligibility check was the outlier.
> > 
> > This preserves Mel's intended fix: don't scan stuff the user explicitly
> > pinned. But allow default policy vmas to participate in balancing.
> > 
> > Reported-by: Neha Gholkar <nehagholkar@gmail.com>
> > Tested-by: Neha Gholkar <nehagholkar@gmail.com>
> > Fixes: fc3147245d19 ("mm: numa: Limit NUMA scanning to migrate-on-fault VMAs")
> > Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> 
> Reviewed-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

Thanks! Sorry for making you feel bad.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu` function to route RPC transports to a populated pool instead of one that may be empty due to uneven thread distribution across pools. This ensures that incoming connections are always serviced, even if some pools have no threads assigned to them. The change trades off NUMA locality for guaranteed serviceability of transports.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes such as SVC_POOL_AUTO/GLOBAL/PERCPU. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts is changed to create one pool per NUMA node by default. This change simplifies the code and improves performance by providing proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any value other than 'pernode' will emit a deprecation notice.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that each CPU-bearing node in a NUMA system has at least one thread when distributing RPC threads automatically. When the requested number of threads is less than the number of pools, some nodes might have empty pools due to even distribution. This patch floors each pool's thread count at one if it belongs to a CPU-bearing node, preventing transports from being steered to empty pools. The total number of threads may exceed the requested count as a result. This change only affects automatic thread distribution and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set by callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] sunrpc: eliminate a modulus operation from the enqueueing codepath
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the sunrpc enqueueing code by replacing a costly modulus operation with an explicit check for array bounds. The original code used a modulus to determine which pool to enqueue on, but this has been replaced with a simple branch that checks if the index is within the valid range. If it's not, the code falls back to pool 0. This change reduces the number of cycles required and improves performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series hardcodes the pool mode to 'pernode' and removes other modes. It addresses a shortcoming in the existing code where RPCs can be scheduled to pools with no threads, by choosing another pool that does have threads if necessary. The patches also include performance optimizations and improvements to thread auto-placement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't let shrinker touch extent_maps that are being logged

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't let shrinker touch extent_maps that are being logged

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Btrfs extent map shrinker that can cause corruption and RCU stalls by freeing an extent map that is still being logged. The fix involves adding a check to skip extent maps that are being logged, similar to how pinned maps are skipped. This prevents a use-after-free situation where a later fsync operation tries to access the freed extent map. The patch addresses a problem that has been observed in production environments on both ARM64 and x86_64 architectures.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned the patch's logic, pointing out that fsync takes the inode's i_mmap_lock in write/exclusive mode and btrfs_scan_inode also takes this lock in shared/read mode, making it unlikely for the shrinker to race with fsync. He also corrected a minor syntax error.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't see how that can happen, because fsync always takes the
inode's i_mmap_lock (in write/exclusive mode), and btrfs_scan_inode()
takes the same lock too (in shared/read mode).
EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING is only set and cleared during fsync, so how can
the shrinker race with fsync?

Also, there should be no () after modified_extents (it's not a function name).

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the i_mmap_lock interlock is incomplete and can't guarantee that LOGGING won't be set on extent maps after taking the lock, which contradicts the original invariant. The author provided analysis showing that tree-log.c never takes i_mmap_lock, but btrfs_sync_file does only for the inode being fsync'd, leaving other inodes vulnerable to having their ems' LOGGING flag set while the shrinker is running.
review_comments.raw_body:
...and yet, the vmcore shows that the shrinker hit an extent_map that
had LOGGING set while holding the i_mmap_lock.

I had hoped to add an assertion here that LOGGING couldn't be set on
the em's after taking the i_mmap_lock, but that's not viable:

The problem is that the i_mmap_lock coverage isn't complete. I had
Claude assist me with some analysis so take it with a hefty grain of
salt but the problem is that the em tree can contain em's from other
inodes where the i_mmap_lock is not held:

-----------------------8<---------------------

...that inode is in progress, therefore no em in its tree should have LOGGING set. Your invariant is the right one.

 But it's violated, because the i_mmap_lock interlock is incomplete. Two facts:                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  1. tree-log.c never takes i_mmap_lock — grep returns 0 occurrences. Only btrfs_sync_file takes it (file.c:1608/1610), and only                                                                                         
  on the one inode being fsync'd.                                                                                                                                                                                        
  2. A single fsync logs many other inodes, and each can set LOGGING on its own ems via btrfs_log_changed_extents().                                                                                                     
  btrfs_log_inode() is called recursively on conflicting inodes, parent directories, and referenced inodes:                                                                                                              
  tree-log.c:5982  btrfs_log_inode(di_inode, log_mode)        # dir dentries                                                                                                                                             
  tree-log.c:6339  btrfs_log_inode(inode, LOG_INODE_ALL)      # conflicting inodes                                                                                                                                       
  tree-log.c:6900  btrfs_log_inode(di_inode, log_mode)        # new dir dentries                                                                                                                                         
  tree-log.c:7389  btrfs_log_inode(dir_inode, LOG_INODE_ALL)  # log_all_parents                                                                                                                                          
  tree-log.c:7488  btrfs_log_inode(inode, ...)                # referenced inodes                                                                                                                                        

  2. For all of these, btrfs_sync_file holds i_mmap_lock on the original fsync'd inode — not on these side inodes. So a side                                                                                             
  inode X gets LOGGING set on its ems while X's own i_mmap_lock is free.                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  So the shrinker can down_read_trylock(X->i_mmap_lock) successfully (nobody holds it) and walk X's tree while X's ems are                                                                                               
  LOGGING-flagged → it removes+frees one → corruption. The interlock only protects the directly-fsync'd inode, not the inodes                                                                                            
  logged as its dependencies.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the em tree should only contain extent maps for its owning inode, and suggested investigating why the buggy behavior occurs elsewhere in the code
review_comments.raw_body:
And that is the problem: an em tree, which belongs to an inode, should
only have extent maps for that inode...
An extent map can only belong to an inode.

So that's what needs investigation. The bug is elsewhere.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that while a single fsync can log other inodes without taking their i_mmap_lock, the extents are not logged due to logging being done in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, except for symlink inodes which are always logged in full mode and have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.

Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned the patch's assumption that extent maps being logged would be skipped by the shrinker, pointing out that there is no actual code path where an inode being fsynced in LOG_INODE_ALL mode would log another inode. He noted an exception for symlinks, which always have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.
review_comments.raw_body:
A single fsync can log other inodes yes, and without taking their
i_mmap_lock, but the inodes are logged in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, so
extents are not logged.
The only exception is for symlink inodes, but those are logged in full
mode, only have one extent (inlined), and always have
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents() for symlinks.

---

Nop. Unless you can show an actual code path where while fsyncing one
inode we log another inode in LOG_INODE_ALL mode
Again, the exception is symlinks, but for those, the
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC flag is always set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents().

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: don't let shrinker touch extent_maps that are being logged
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Btrfs extent map shrinker that can cause corruption and RCU stalls by freeing an extent map that is still being logged. The fix involves adding a check to skip extent maps that are being logged, similar to how pinned maps are skipped. This prevents a use-after-free situation where a later fsync operation tries to access the freed extent map. The patch addresses a problem that has been observed in production environments on both ARM64 and x86_64 architectures.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the em tree should only contain extent maps for its owning inode, and suggested investigating why the buggy behavior occurs elsewhere in the code
review_comments.raw_body:
And that is the problem: an em tree, which belongs to an inode, should
only have extent maps for that inode...
An extent map can only belong to an inode.

So that's what needs investigation. The bug is elsewhere.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that while a single fsync can log other inodes without taking their i_mmap_lock, the extents are not logged due to logging being done in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, except for symlink inodes which are always logged in full mode and have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.

Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned the patch's assumption that extent maps being logged would be skipped by the shrinker, pointing out that there is no actual code path where an inode being fsynced in LOG_INODE_ALL mode would log another inode. He noted an exception for symlinks, which always have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.
review_comments.raw_body:
A single fsync can log other inodes yes, and without taking their
i_mmap_lock, but the inodes are logged in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, so
extents are not logged.
The only exception is for symlink inodes, but those are logged in full
mode, only have one extent (inlined), and always have
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents() for symlinks.

---

Nop. Unless you can show an actual code path where while fsyncing one
inode we log another inode in LOG_INODE_ALL mode
Again, the exception is symlinks, but for those, the
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC flag is always set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents().

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 0/3] iomap: convert to in-iter ->iomap_next() model

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 0/3] iomap: convert to in-iter ->iomap_next() model

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 1/3] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function called ->iomap_next() to the iomap iteration mechanism, which replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks. The new callback allows performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback, improving efficiency. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to simplify the logic needed in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change enables filesystems to be converted one at a time from using the legacy ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() path to the new ->iomap_next() callback.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested adding function typedefs for consistency and questioned the need to pass separate iomap and srcmap arguments, instead suggesting passing only the iter argument.
review_comments.raw_body:
If you add function typedefs for all the other two methods I'd one one here
as well for consistency.  We'll need it anywone once the legacy ops are
entirely gone we can kill of the ops and just pass this function pointer.

As for the arguments:  I don't really see much of a point in passing
the iomap/scrmap separately vs just the iter.  Or am I missing something
here?

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential issue with callback modifying internal fields, agreed to simplify iomap_next() by dropping two arguments and making it non-const.
review_comments.raw_body:
It was meant to safeguard against a callback modifying iomap-internal
non-mapping fields (iter->pos/len/status/iter_start_pos) but maybe
this is unnecessary. I'll drop the 2 args and make it non-const.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the iomap_iter() function should maintain its current version without changes to constness, but expressed concern about potential future issues with non-const data in the iter private structure.
review_comments.raw_body:
As in my reply to Gao I missed the constness point.  So I think keeping
the current version should be ok.  I'm a little worried that we might
end up with something not beeing const in the end like the iter private
data, though.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 2/3] xfs: convert read and buffered write iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the read and buffered write iomap operations for XFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which simplifies the code by removing redundant functions and leveraging the iomap_process helper function. The change does not introduce any functional changes but rather refactors the existing implementation. This is a step towards migrating all XFS iomap operations to use the ->iomap_next() callback.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 3/3] xfs: example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch is an example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks in the XFS file system. It removes indirect calls by inlining callback functions and allows the compiler to make direct calls, reducing overhead. The patch modifies several files, including iomap/buffered-io.c, iomap/iter.c, xfs/xfs_file.c, and others, adding new functions and modifying existing ones to use the devirtualized callbacks. The goal is to improve performance by eliminating indirect function calls.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested exploring devirtualization on the direct I/O path to further improve performance

Reviewer suggested creating a helper function or integrating the initialization code into iomap_buffered_write_inline() to reduce duplication.

Reviewer noted that the patch is a good start but does not implement the next scheme as claimed in the commit message.

Reviewer requested reduction of boilerplate code in the patch
review_comments.raw_body:
Nice.  Btw, a more critical path would be direct I/O.

Fengnan, maybe you can play around with this series and your direct read
fast path?

---

We'll probably want a helper for this initializion boilerplate.
Or make it part of iomap_buffered_write_inline somehow?

---

So we're not even doing the _next scheme here and already get
results, nice!

---

Similarly here, it would be great if we could somehow avoid
too much boilerplate.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to restructure the code in v2 by moving all callbacks into iomap_buffered_write_inline() and removing the indirect call, confirming that a fix is planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, I think this can all just be moved into
iomap_buffered_write_inline() and then the call would just be:

  ret = iomap_buffered_write_inline(iocb, from,
                xfs_buffered_write_iomap_begin, xfs_buffered_write_iomap_end,
                &xfs_iomap_write_ops, NULL);

I'll clean this up for v2.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Fengnan
review_comments.summary: Fengnan reported that enabling iomap_next mode improves performance by 1.6% when used alone, but the gain drops to some extent after enabling it in combination with simple read; they are still investigating potential issues in their modification of iomap_dio_rw.
review_comments.raw_body:
First, a brief update on my progress: I modified |iomap_dio_rw| so that 
the XFS direct

I/O read path can support |iomap_next| mode, and ran 4K random read 
performance tests.

Using the performance with both |iomap_next| and simple read disabled as 
the baseline,

enabling only |iomap_next| improved performance by 1.6%; enabling only 
simple read

improved performance by 7.3%; enabling both |iomap_next| and simple read

improved performance by 8.8%.

In non-|iomap_next| mode, |simple read| improves performance by about 10%.
After enabling |iomap_next|, the gain drops somewhat. The changes needed 
to make
|iomap_dio_rw| support |iomap_next| are relatively large, so there may 
be issues in my
current modification. I am still investigating why.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iomap code to use an in-iter model, where a single callback function is used for both beginning and ending I/O operations. The conversion involves merging two existing functions into one and adding a helper function to process I/O operations. An example of devirtualizing the callbacks on a hot path is also provided to avoid indirect calls through struct iomap_ops. The patch series raises questions about whether this conversion is worth it, given that indirect calls are cheap on modern hardware.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer expressed strong support for the patch's direction of converting to an in-iter ->iomap_next() model, citing its potential to make iomap more flexible as a file system I/O library.
review_comments.raw_body:
As I mentioned a year ago, I really hope this way can be proceed
to avoid iomap iter-callback models:

https://lore.kernel.org/r/20250905152118.GE1587915@frogsfrogsfrogs

And in that way, iomap can be more flexible as a fs IO library.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that avoiding indirect calls is not primarily for performance or security reasons, but rather to give filesystems more flexibility in controlling their IO paths by allowing them to customize the iomap callbacks.
review_comments.raw_body:
IMHO, hhe main point is not to avoid the indirect call to make it
noticeably faster or to avoid security issues due to
Spectre/Meltdown/...

It just gives more flexibility to specific filesystems to control
the IO path better (since each filesystem might have its own
slightly different IO flow), rather than adding various flags/code
in the iomap core itself to fulfill the current callback model.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that for consistency, function typedefs should be added for the ->iomap_next() method, and also questioned the necessity of passing iomap/scrmap separately from iter.
review_comments.raw_body:
If you add function typedefs for all the other two methods I'd one one here
as well for consistency.  We'll need it anywone once the legacy ops are
entirely gone we can kill of the ops and just pass this function pointer.

As for the arguments:  I don't really see much of a point in passing
the iomap/scrmap separately vs just the iter.  Or am I missing something
here?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
reviewer suggested exploring the conversion of iomap to in-iter model on a more critical path, specifically direct I/O, and asked Fengnan to experiment with this series on their direct read fast path

reviewer suggested simplifying initialization boilerplate by either introducing a new helper function or integrating it into the existing iomap_buffered_write_inline function

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig expressed positive sentiment towards the patch, stating that it is already achieving good results without fully implementing the ->iomap_next() model.

reviewer expressed a desire to minimize boilerplate code in the patch series
review_comments.raw_body:
Nice.  Btw, a more critical path would be direct I/O.

Fengnan, maybe you can play around with this series and your direct read
fast path?

---

We'll probably want a helper for this initializion boilerplate.
Or make it part of iomap_buffered_write_inline somehow?

---

So we're not even doing the _next scheme here and already get
results, nice!

---

Similarly here, it would be great if we could somehow avoid
too much boilerplate.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that passing the iomap and srcmap separately requires being able to pass a const iter, which is not currently possible
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh, I guess this is where passing the iomap and srcmap separately
from the iter come from.  But for this to make sense we'd have to
be able to pass a const iter.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: reviewer agrees that avoiding indirect calls is beneficial and considers the conversion to in-iter model as a win-win situation
review_comments.raw_body:
Indirect calls have always been relatively slow, even without spectre.
So if we can easily avoid them that's always a win.  That doesn't mean we
should do stupid things just to avoid them, but I think we have a win/win
here.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gao Xiang expressed concerns that the callback model in the patch may not scale well for future iomap users, suggesting consideration of a non-callback model as an alternative direction.
review_comments.raw_body:
... anyway, I hope iomap folks could consider my humble
suggestion, since I'm afraid that this callback model in
the long term won't scale well in very slightly different
details (along with more and more iomap users), so rather
than just converting more callers, I guess it might be
better to consider non-callback model as a direction
anyway.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to restructure the code in v2 by moving the iomap_buffered_write_inline() call into a single function, addressing Christoph's feedback about simplifying the buffered write path.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, I think this can all just be moved into
iomap_buffered_write_inline() and then the call would just be:

  ret = iomap_buffered_write_inline(iocb, from,
                xfs_buffered_write_iomap_begin, xfs_buffered_write_iomap_end,
                &xfs_iomap_write_ops, NULL);

I'll clean this up for v2.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that passing two arguments to iomap_next() was unnecessary, agreed to remove them and make the function non-const in a future version.
review_comments.raw_body:
It was meant to safeguard against a callback modifying iomap-internal
non-mapping fields (iter->pos/len/status/iter_start_pos) but maybe
this is unnecessary. I'll drop the 2 args and make it non-const.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Joanne Koong is addressing Gao Xiang's feedback about the iomap_next() model, specifically the difference between her approach and Gao's proposal to have callers own the iter and drive the loop to get rid of indirect calls. She agrees that her patch does what Gao had in mind but disagrees with moving the loop body logic out into helper functions, citing duplicate code and lack of benefit.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks Gao. I don't think the callback and helper models are as far
apart as it might look though after the ->iomap_next() change. Once
all the callers have been converted, there'll be no iomap_ops
indirection left and callers can just pass in the direct function
pointer for iomap_next(). You had mentioned something about having
callers own the iter and drive the loop to get rid of indirect calls -
I think the inline patch in this series does what you had in mind.

I think the difference is that under your proposal, the loop body
logic for pre/postprocessing the read/write/etc ranges is also moved
out into helper functions, which each caller would then have to put
together / reassemble. It seems like a lot of duplicate code and I'm
not sure I see the benefit of open-coding that when none of the
callers right now need special logic interjected between the
fine-grained helpers and and their callback. imo it seems more
advantageous to only add that in the future if there's a caller that
has that special need, where only that caller would need to do that,
instead of making all callers have to do that.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the patch introduces a potential deadlock scenario where iomap_next() is called while holding the folio lock, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap().

reviewer noted that the patch does not fully align with their original in-iter direction and provided a specific example of the design model difference they are concerned about

Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the recent iomap implementation is still callback-based and requires arranging mapping logic into unique callbacks, which can be inflexible for filesystems needing additional information. He suggested a high-level fs helper library to wrap up page cache management, IO management, and other operations, allowing for more flexibility in handling different types of I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 2026/6/26 05:33, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Thanks for the reply.

I partially agree since it's much closer, but what I meant is
the design model difference as below.

---

If you take a look at the initial commit of iomap ae259a9c8593
("fs: introduce iomap infrastructure"), the starting point was
to provide `struct iomap_ops` (two callbacks) to get the filesystem
extent mapping and unique entrypoints: iomap_file_buffered_write(),
iomap_page_mkwrite() for buffer writes (write and mmap write).

And then iomap applies to DAX, direct I/O, writeback, buffer read,
etc.  basically for block-based filesystems, and that is why
`struct iomap_ops` is always _one important part of iomap core_
until FUSE adapted.


But if we take a look at the overall requirement, I think what we
need is to provide an easy-use high-level fs helper library to
wrap up the following stuffs:

  1. page cache management (mainly sub-page tracking) and DAX pages;

  2. IO management (buffer I/O, direct I/O, DAX access);

  3. others (like fiemap, bmap, lseek, etc.)


For example, most block filesystems could use them of all, and for
FUSE and networking filesystem, basically they only need 1 and
maybe partial 3.

The recent implementation (including ->iomap_next()), is still
a callback-based model, the main loop is inside `fs/iomap` itself,
and you have to:

  - still arrange the mapping logic into an unique callback, which
is .iomap_next() now, if the mapping logic needs more information
rather than `struct inode *`, they have to add private pointer
somewhere.

  - Take a recent example (buffered read refactoring), need
    `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` for FUSE and maybe other future
    networking filesystems to callback into their filesystem to
    provide new I/O contexts and submit I/Os.

But actually take a simple example of iomap_read_folio, you:
could change into (just a very rough sketch):

  xxxfs_read_folio {
    struct xxxxfs_private priv;

    iomap_iter(&iter) {   /// it could be a macro
        if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);

        // to maintain the page cache <-> struct iomap_folio_state
        // and handle some type of mapping
        if (iomap_read_folio_iter(&iter) == NEED_NEW_IO_CONTEXT) {
            xxxfs_new_io (&iter, &priv);
        }
    }
    if (xxxfs_if_any_available_io(&iter, &priv))
	xxxfs_submit_io(&iter, &priv);
}

In this model, I really think `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` and
similar doesn't need any more (another set of callback avoidness),
and both xxxfs_map_extent() and xxxfs_new_io()/ xxxfs_submit_io()
can pass in their per-fs private without need to bother
the iomap-core to add various `private` pointers.

and in the iomap_iter() loop you can omit/change/add any part if
it doesn't fulfill to the filesystem timing.

As for "a lot of duplicate code", I think with proper new designs,
the common part could be wrapped up into macros (for example,
for bdev filesystems, xxxfs_new_io() and xxxfs_submit_io() calls
can be wrapped up into macros, and only leave:

	if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);"

can be customized for each fs as the common macro helpers.

It's just my rough thought, as I said in the previous year, I
still don't have a strong tendency but it's my suggestion over
these years.

Anyway since you're actively working/changing on iomap, so
it's fine to just respect your ideas/efforts if you don't
think it's worth, but as a record of my thoughts here again.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig expressed concerns about the complexity and benefits of converting to an in-iter ->iomap_next() model, suggesting a more incremental approach by first moving to a single callback, then inline for performance-critical paths, and finally attempting to convert to a pure iterator. He requested a proof-of-concept from Joanne Koong to gauge interest.
review_comments.raw_body:
This does sound like a lot of work, and I'm not a 100% sold on
the benefits, although getting rid of callbacks would be nice.

I'd be tempted to say that as a first step we should move the the
single callback, as a second stop (after converting everyone to
the single callback) to inline for performance critical path, and
then see if we can massage it into a pure iterator without much
downside instead of trying to do everything at once.

As you seem to have the strongest opinion here, I'd love to see
a proof of concept from you to get everyone excited.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current patch does not address the constness issue and is concerned that the iter private data might not remain constant
review_comments.raw_body:
As in my reply to Gao I missed the constness point.  So I think keeping
the current version should be ok.  I'm a little worried that we might
end up with something not beeing const in the end like the iter private
data, though.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where iomap_next() returns an error, and requested a check for this condition before calling try_to_unmap()

Reviewer Gao Xiang expressed interest in the patch but noted that it's not a blocker for their current use cases, and suggested exploring its application to other filesystems like btrfs and f2fs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Christoph,

On 2026/6/26 13:10, Christoph Hellwig wrote:

---

I know it's much better to "show the code" but I also think it's
useful to comment ideas now rather than later in case that anyone
is interested so that it could be useful for their adaption.
I hope I could arrange it in my formal todo list, but I've already
had a bunch of other things (erofs fsdax dedupe support as I told
to you since it impacts workloads, and some overlayfs improvement
and more) in my scheduling list.

Although I tend to play with this idea, it's not a blocker on
my main interested use cases for now.  Also see if some
complicated conversion like btrfs and f2fs can make more related
developers interested in following this way.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig requested that the patch series focus on converting iomap operations from begin/end to an iter model, citing that it is simpler than expected and would allow for a smoother transition before moving on to inlining callbacks.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah.  But for now I'd like to get the relatively low hanging fruit
done.  And Joanne showed that moving from begin/end to an iter is
actually way simpler than I thought by having the iomap_process
helper that still does get callbacks, but which get inlined.  So
I'd be really happy to get this done ASAP before we get a lot more
conversions, and also to avoid having a partial transitition where
we need to keep the old ops around for too long.

After that doing the inlining as in patch 3 becomes pretty easy,
so we can do it where it matter like direct I/O.  And also once
we are down to a single callback I think we have a better baseline
for your idea.

review_comments.author: Fengnan
review_comments.summary: Fengnan reported that enabling iomap_next mode improves performance by 1.6% when used alone, but the gain drops to around 10% after enabling simple read as well; he is still investigating issues in his current modification to support iomap_next in iomap_dio_rw
review_comments.raw_body:
First, a brief update on my progress: I modified |iomap_dio_rw| so that 
the XFS direct

I/O read path can support |iomap_next| mode, and ran 4K random read 
performance tests.

Using the performance with both |iomap_next| and simple read disabled as 
the baseline,

enabling only |iomap_next| improved performance by 1.6%; enabling only 
simple read

improved performance by 7.3%; enabling both |iomap_next| and simple read

improved performance by 8.8%.

In non-|iomap_next| mode, |simple read| improves performance by about 10%.
After enabling |iomap_next|, the gain drops somewhat. The changes needed 
to make
|iomap_dio_rw| support |iomap_next| are relatively large, so there may 
be issues in my
current modification. I am still investigating why.
